explanation: SET NOCOUNT OFF leaves row-count messages enabled for every statement, adding needless network chatter for callers.
SET XACT_ABORT OFF while the procedure later opens transactions allows run-time errors to leave a transaction partially applied.
SELECT * on Sales.Orders couples callers to all current and future columns, including wide nvarchar(MAX) comment columns.
SELECT TOP (10) from Sales.Orders without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary, non-deterministic set of rows.
DISTINCT on o.OrderID is redundant because OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders and already unique.
WITH (NOLOCK) on Sales.Orders can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated rows under concurrent modification.
YEAR(o.OrderDate) in the predicate prevents an index range seek on OrderDate; use OrderDate >= '20160101' AND OrderDate < '20170101' instead.
Comparing int column o.CustomerID with nvarchar variable @CustomerIdText forces an implicit conversion that can degrade the plan.
The pattern (@CustomerID IS NULL OR o.CustomerID = @CustomerID) defeats index seeks and produces poor cardinality estimates; use OPTION (RECOMPILE) or dynamic branching.
LIKE N'%urgent%' on o.Comments starts with a wildcard, forcing a scan instead of an index seek.
NOT IN against a subquery returns no rows if the subquery ever yields NULL; prefer NOT EXISTS for safe semantics.
ISNULL(o.CustomerID, 0) wraps an indexed column and makes the predicate non-sargable; CustomerID is NOT NULL so the wrapper is also redundant.
o.OrderID + 0 = @OrderID performs arithmetic on the key column, blocking a direct index seek on OrderID.
o.CustomerID <> 1 is an inequality predicate that typically scans most of Sales.Orders rather than seeking.
SELECT TOP (1) over the cross join has no ORDER BY, so the returned row is arbitrary.
CROSS JOIN between Sales.Orders and Sales.Customers has no join predicate and multiplies row counts of both tables.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary order row.
Comma-separated FROM list of Sales.Orders and Sales.OrderLines with the join condition in WHERE; use explicit INNER JOIN syntax.
The scalar COUNT(*) subquery against Sales.OrderLines executes per outer order row; rewrite as a GROUP BY join or OUTER APPLY.
Correlated EXISTS against Sales.OrderLines runs per outer row and could be expressed as a semi-join / set join on OrderID.
The CASE WHEN o.Comments IS NULL expression is duplicated in the select list and ORDER BY; reference the alias or compute once.
ORDER BY 2 references a select-list position and silently changes meaning if columns are reordered; use ORDER BY o.OrderDate.
OFFSET/FETCH ordered only by o.OrderDate, which is not unique; add a tie-breaker such as OrderID to make pages stable.
UNION between branches filtered on OrderID = 1 and OrderID = 2 is disjoint by construction; UNION ALL avoids a needless duplicate-removal sort.
(SELECT COUNT(*) ...) > 0 counts all matching order lines when EXISTS would stop at the first match.
SELECT @OrderID = o.OrderID from a multi-row table silently keeps the last row's value; use SET with a deterministic single-row query.
Table variable @OrderIds can receive a fixed low cardinality estimate, producing poor plans if it holds non-trivial row counts; consider a temp table.
INSERT ... SELECT TOP (5) from Sales.Orders without ORDER BY captures an arbitrary five rows.
SELECT ... INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temp table schema implicitly; declare it explicitly with CREATE TABLE for stable typing.
SELECT TOP (5) without ORDER BY populates #ImplicitOrders with arbitrary rows.
Cursor order_cursor with OPEN/FETCH NEXT processes Sales.Orders row by row; this work can be expressed set-wise.
The cursor's SELECT TOP (2) has no ORDER BY, so the fetched rows are non-deterministic.
WHILE loop performs iterative row-at-a-time style processing instead of a single set-based statement.
@CustomerID is converted and concatenated into the SQL text; bind it as a parameter with sys.sp_executesql instead.
EXEC (@Sql) executes a concatenated string with no parameterization, preventing plan reuse.
@OrderID is embedded into the SQL string by concatenation rather than being passed as a bound parameter.
sys.sp_executesql is called without a parameter list; the value is baked into the statement text, defeating parameterization.
CREATE TABLE #Scratch is never dropped before the procedure ends, leaving it to implicit cleanup at scope exit.
The CATCH block only selects ERROR_MESSAGE() and neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status, hiding the error from callers.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path; prefer THROW, which preserves severity and original error semantics.
PRINT output is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostics; use structured logging or RAISERROR ... WITH NOWAIT if needed.
Business identifier CustomerID = 1 is hard-coded in the query instead of being passed as a parameter or looked up.
Unlabelled date constant '20160101' is embedded in the filter on o.OrderDate; give it a named parameter or documented variable.
CONVERT(date, '01/02/2016') has no style parameter; the result depends on session DATEFORMAT/language and can flip day and month.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row from the join.
COALESCE on CustomerID in the ON clause prevents normal index-based join optimization between Orders and Invoices; both columns are NOT NULL anyway.
SELECT TOP (1) without ORDER BY returns an arbitrary row.
Sales.Orders is self-joined on OrderID only to return the same key already available in the outer row.
ORDER BY o.Comments sorts every order by an nvarchar(MAX) LOB column, causing an expensive, unselective wide sort.
SUM over the entire Sales.OrderLines fact-like table with no restrictive filter scans all rows for one scalar.
ol.OrderID > 0 is a row-level predicate placed in HAVING; moving it to WHERE filters rows before grouping.
ORDER BY on a single-row aggregate (COUNT(*) without GROUP BY) is meaningless overhead.
MERGE on Sales.Orders has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases; use separate IF EXISTS / UPDATE / INSERT blocks per the style guide.
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET target.Comments = target.Comments assigns the column to itself, causing logging and locking without changing data.
UPDATE ... SET Comments = Comments writes the current value back, generating log records and locks with no effect.
DELETE from Sales.OrderLines filtered only on Quantity = -1, a broad non-key business value rather than a stable key predicate.
WAITFOR DELAY '00:00:01' blocks a worker thread inside the procedure instead of using asynchronous scheduling.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is opened outside a TRY/CATCH block, so an error could leave the transaction open or in an undefined state.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT Sales.Orders ON changes session identity-management state and creates fragile coupling to the table's identity column.
EXEC sp_who calls a system routine without schema qualification; use EXEC sys.sp_who to avoid resolution overhead and ambiguity.
SET ROWCOUNT 1 changes a session-level setting affecting all subsequent statements and is never restored to 0.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories VALUES (...) omits the column list, so the statement breaks silently if the table's column order changes.
INSERT INTO #DemoCustomers relies on implicit column ordering with no explicit column list.
LIKE '%' + @CustomerName + '%' begins with a wildcard, preventing a seek on the unique CustomerName index.
@CustomerName is concatenated directly into the dynamic SQL string instead of being bound as an sp_executesql parameter.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated into executable SQL text, allowing SQL injection; bind it via sp_executesql parameters.
sp_executesql is invoked without a parameter definition list; the user value is embedded in the string rather than parameterized.
sp_executesql is called without a schema qualifier; use sys.sp_executesql.
UPDATE #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and overwrites CustomerCategoryID for every row in the table.
Business value CustomerCategoryID = 1 is hard-coded instead of parameterized (e.g., using @NewCategory lookup).
WHERE o.OrderDate >= '2016-01-01' filters the LEFT JOINed Sales.Orders table, silently converting the outer join to an inner join; move the predicate to the ON clause if outer semantics are intended.
Unlabelled date constant '2016-01-01' is embedded directly in the join filter logic.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL never evaluates to TRUE under default ANSI_NULLS; use DeliveryMethodID IS NULL.
Scalar UDF dbo.GetCustomerType(CustomerID) in the select list forces row-by-row evaluation and inhibits parallelism.
The CATCH block handles an error raised inside an open transaction but never issues ROLLBACK, risking an orphaned open transaction.
The CATCH block returns ERROR_MESSAGE() as a result set but neither rethrows with THROW nor returns a failure status.
WITH (INDEX = 1) hard-codes an index choice on Sales.Customers, overriding the optimizer and breaking if indexes are reorganized.
Three levels of nested derived tables (t1/t2/t3) obscure the logic; refactor into a CTE or a single flat query.
LIKE '%A%' on CustomerName begins with a wildcard, forcing a scan of the unique CustomerName index.
SELECT * against dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti couples the caller to every column the function returns.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti is a multi-statement TVF, which acts as an optimization barrier with fixed cardinality estimates; an inline TVF would be optimized with the outer query.
